perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated without the payment of any penalty by either party upon sixty (60) days' written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated without the payment of any penalty by either party upon fifteen (15) days' written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 60 days to 15 days could be problematic under certain interpretations of contract law, particularly if it's deemed an unreasonably short period that doesn't allow sufficient time for transition or finding a replacement service provider. This may violate general principles of fairness and reasonableness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no specific statute mandating a *minimum* notice period in service contracts under Massachusetts law,  MGL c. 106, § 1-309 states that the time for taking any action is to be a 'reasonable time.'  Fifteen days may be deemed unreasonable in the context of investment advisory services, especially if it causes undue hardship on the other party. The modified text contradicts this general requirement for reasonableness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article1, Section 1-309 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-309 Option To Accelerate At Will Section 1-309 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1-309: Option To Accelerate At Will Section 1–309. A term providing that 1 party or that party's successor in interest may accelerate payment or performance or require collateral or additional collateral ''at will'' or when the party ''deems itself insecure'', or words of similar import, means that the party has power to do so only if that party in good faith believes that the prospect of payment or performance is impaired. The burden of establishing lack of good faith is on the party against which the power has been exercised. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the termination notice period from 60 to 15 days, which the law explanation argues may be deemed unreasonable under MGL c. 106, § 1-309, which states that the time for taking any action is to be a 'reasonable time.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Section 1-309 of the General Laws, supporting this claim. The reduced notice period could cause undue hardship, directly contradicting the requirement for a 'reasonable time' as interpreted under Massachusetts law, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: For the performance of the Adviser's obligations under this Agreement, including payment of certain expenses of each of the Funds pursuant to paragraph 3 hereof, each Fund shall pay the Adviser, on the first business day following the end of each month, a fee at an annual rate of 0.65% of a Fund's average daily net assets up to $25 million, 0.35% of a Fund's average daily net assets from $25 million to $100 million, and 0.25% of a Fund's average daily net assets for such assets in excess of $100 million.
perturbation.changed_text: For the performance of the Adviser's obligations under this Agreement, including payment of certain expenses of each of the Funds pursuant to paragraph 3 hereof, each Fund shall pay the Adviser, at the Adviser's discretion, a fee at an annual rate of 0.65% of a Fund's average daily net assets up to $25 million, 0.35% of a Fund's average daily net assets from $25 million to $100 million, and 0.25% of a Fund's average daily net assets for such assets in excess of $100 million.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment timing from 'on the first business day following the end of each month' to 'at the Adviser's discretion' introduces ambiguity and potential violation of fiduciary duty.  Mutual fund advisers have a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of the fund and its shareholders.  Delaying or manipulating the timing of payments without a legitimate reason can be a breach of that duty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Investment Company Act of 1940 - Section 36(a) Breach of Fiduciary Duty
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-35 - Breach of fiduciary duty
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 36(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 imposes a fiduciary duty on investment advisers.  Giving the Adviser sole discretion over payment timing, without specifying any constraints or guidelines, creates an opportunity for the Adviser to prioritize its own interests over the Fund's, potentially violating its fiduciary duty to act in the best interest of the fund's shareholders. By potentially withholding payments or delaying them without a valid reason it could be seen as acting counter to the funds best interest.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-35 - Breach of fiduciary duty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-35 - Breach of fiduciary duty U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Civil actions by Commission; jurisdiction; allegations; injunctive or other relief The Commission is authorized to bring an action in the proper district court of the United States, or in the United States court of any territory or other place subject to the jurisdiction of the United States, alleging that a person who is, or at the time of the alleged misconduct was, serving or acting in one or more of the following capacities has engaged within five years of the commencement of the action or is about to engage in any act or practice constituting a breach of fiduciary duty involving personal misconduct in respect of any registered investment company for which such person so serves or acts, or at the time of the alleged misconduct, so served or acted— (1) as officer, director , member of any advisory board , investment adviser , or depositor; or (2) as principal underwriter, if such registered company is an open-end company , unit investment trust , or face-amount certificate company . If such allegations are established, the court may enjoin such persons from acting in any or all such capacities either permanently or temporarily and award such injunctive or other relief against such person as may be reasonable and appropriate in the circumstances, having due regard to the protection of investors and to the effectuation of the policies declared in section 80a–1(b) of this title . (b) Compensation or payments as basis of fiduciary duty; civil actions by Commission or security holder; burden of proof; judicial consideration of director or shareholder approval; persons liable; extent of liability; exempted transactions; jurisdiction; finding restriction For the purposes of this s
perturbation.justification: The changed text grants the Adviser discretion over payment timing, potentially conflicting with their fiduciary duty under the Investment Company Act of 1940. The original text specifies a fixed payment schedule ('on the first business day following the end of each month'), ensuring timely payments. The modified text removes this obligation, allowing the Adviser to delay payments at their discretion. This creates a conflict of interest because the Adviser could prioritize its own interests over the Fund's shareholders by withholding or delaying payments. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 15 U.S. Code § 80a-35, addresses 'Breach of fiduciary duty,' indicating the legal significance of this obligation. While the snippet doesn't detail specifics, the potential for self-serving behavior contradicts the fiduciary duty implied in the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Adviser agrees to defend and indemnify the Trust, and its trustees, officers and agent from and against any and all liability, losses, claims and damages arising from any inaccurate or incomplete statement provided by the Adviser (or its officers, employees, directors and agents) for inclusion in the Fund's prospectus or statement of additional information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser agrees to defend and indemnify the Trust, and its trustees, officers and agent from and against any and all liability, losses, claims and damages arising from any inaccurate statement provided by the Adviser (or its officers, employees, directors and agents) for inclusion in the Fund's prospectus or statement of additional information, provided that the trust immediately notifies the advisor within 24 hours of the statement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a requirement for immediate notification (24 hours) for indemnification claims related to inaccurate statements in the prospectus might be considered unreasonably short and could conflict with the statute of limitations or reasonable discovery periods for such claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Act of 1933 - Statute of Limitations for Misleading Statements
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13 of the Securities Act of 1933 (15 U.S.C. § 77m) provides a statute of limitations for actions related to misleading statements in a prospectus. While it does not dictate a specific timeframe for initial *notification*, it provides that actions must be brought within one year after the discovery of the untrue statement or omission, or after such discovery should have been made by the exercise of reasonable diligence. Imposing a 24-hour notification requirement is highly unreasonable, effectively waiving claims even before they could reasonably be discovered, contradicting the intent and protective measures afforded by Section 13.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 77m - Limitation of actions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 77m - Limitation of actions U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next No action shall be maintained to enforce any liability created under section 77k or 77l(a)(2) of this title unless brought within one year after the discovery of the untrue statement or the omission, or after such discovery should have been made by the exercise of reasonable diligence, or, if the action is to enforce a liability created under section 77l(a)(1) of this title , unless brought within one year after the violation upon which it is based. In no event shall any such action be brought to enforce a liability created under section 77k or 77l(a)(1) of this title more than three years after the security was bona fide offered to the public, or under section 77l(a)(2) of this title more than three years after the sale. (May 27, 1933, ch. 38, title I, § 13, 48 Stat. 84 ; June 6, 1934, ch. 404, title II, § 207, 48 Stat. 908 ; Pub. L. 105–353, title III, § 301(a)(3) , Nov. 3, 1998 , 112 Stat. 3235 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 1998— Pub. L. 105–353 substituted “77l(a)(2)” for “77l(2)” in two places and “77l(a)(1)” for “77l(1)” in two places. 1934—Act June 6, 1934 , substituted “one year” for “two years”, “three years” for “ten years”, and inserted “or under section 77l(2) of this title more than three years after the sale”. CFR Title Parts 17 231 U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities How current is this?
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 24-hour notification requirement for inaccurate statements, which contradicts the statute of limitations under Section 13 of the Securities Act of 1933 (15 U.S.C. § 77m). The law explanation states that this requirement is highly unreasonable, potentially waiving claims before they can be reasonably discovered. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 15 U.S. Code § 77m, indicates that actions must be brought within one year after the discovery of the untrue statement or omission, or after such discovery should have been made by the exercise of reasonable diligence. The 24-hour notification significantly undermines this provision by imposing an unreasonably short timeframe, making it an unenforceable and illegal modification.
